channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: Warhammer 40K Eye of Terror Reign of Iron Review - Iron vs Steel
item.content:encoded:
The Eye of Terror, a refuge for those who escaped the Imperium's wrath after the events of the Horus Heresy, and an entrance for assaults against the Imperium for its residents for 10,000 years, is now the source of one of the Traitor Legions' grandest schemes. The Lord of Iron makes his move and sends his deadliest General against the next world holding the line of advance since Cadia's fall, but the Adeptus Mechanicus are ready to defend the world at all costs. Following on from the 500 Worlds Titus and The Maelstrom Lair of the Tyrant campaign books, we check out the next phase of the campaign, Warhammer 40K The Eye of Terror Reign of Iron, along with the new units of the campaign's assaulting legion of Iron Warriors, and its stalwart defenders, the Adeptus Mechanicus.

We've been covering Warhammer 40K for a while on the site, and you can check out our full Warhammer 40K Guide, our preview for the Leviathan box that launched the latest edition of the rules, or the most recent wave of Ultramarines and Space Marine reinforcements.

The Eye of Terror is a massive warp rift where realspace and the madness of the warp join, which allowed the traitor forces of the Chaos Space Marines to escape after the failed assault on Terra during the Horus Heresy and hide out for the millennia since. These forces have launched constant assaults against the Imperium from the Eye, most notably the Black Crusades of Abbadon the Despoiler, son of the arch-traitor Horus.

Other forces have watched these failed campaigns, learning and planning, and now Pertuarbo, Horus' brother, has capitalised on the fall of Cadia and launched his own campaign on the Imperium, starting with the pivotal Forge World of Agripnaa.

The Campaign Books

The fall of Cadia, bastion against incursions from the Eye of Terror, has ignited assaults from multitudes of denzins that were held back by its presence. Daemons and the legions of Traitor, Renegade, and Chaos Space Marines now pour out of the rift. The Eye of Terror Reign of Iron campaign books detail these events, and the start of the Daemon Primarch of the Iron Warriors, Pertuarbo's campaign to conquer Terra, the seat of the Imperium, and finish what he and his brothers failed to do 10,000 years ago.

The Eye of Terror Reign of Iron, like 500 Worlds Titus and The Maelstrom Lair of the Tyrant previously, is a set of campaign books. Unlike the previous 2, it only has 3 books, and doesn't include a campaign book to play through. The first book details the lore and background for the Eye of Terror, and what the fall of Cadia means for the system, along with including details of the Iron Warriors' plans for their campaign, their forces, their target of the world of Agripnaa, and the Adeptus Mechanicus defenders there.

The second book, The Eye of Terror Reign of Iron Apocalypse, includes the rules for fighting massive battles of Warhammer 40K. These games are designed to be played with 3000 or more points, usually a lot more, and with multiple players, each bringing their own collection to add to the huge battlefield. The book includes the rules adaptations for these battles, streaming phases, and adding elements like Fog of War, which means you can't target units with ranged attacks beyond 24 inches during the first turn. This is to stop one side from entirely obliterating their opponents in the first turn, because of the sheer scale of firepower that's usually present in Apocalypse games.

The book also has Apocalypse stratagems for 28 factions, including 5 Space Marines and 4 Chaos Space Marine factions. The book then closes out with 5 Apocalypse missions, and rules for creating your own Apocalypse missions, and running an Apocalypse campaign, if a single Apocalypse game wasn't enough for you.

The final book, The Eye of Terror Reign of Iron - Detachments, is a booklet with new Detachments for Chaos Space Marines, Space Marines, Chaos Knights, Imperial Knights, and the Adeptus Mechanicus. All of these Detachments are available in the downloads section of the Warhammer Community site. Detachments are new with this edition of 40K and give unique special rules for your forces, significantly changing the way factions play between detachment choices, and usually some restrictions on the units you can take.

Full Of Iron

Leading the Iron Warriors campaign to take Agripnaa is one of Pertuarbo's greatest Warsmiths, Kravek Morne. Kravek is a brand new miniature alongside the Eye or Terror Reign of Iron release, and his datacard is as you would expect from a siege-breaker general. Kravek gets 10 attacks in melee across 3 weapons, chief among them being the Last Argument thunder hammer and power fist combo, which has 7 attacks at strength 10. Kravek has a series of abilities that make these even more deadly:

  • Devastating Wounds on the thunder hammer and power fist mean that your opponent can't make saving throws against rolls of 6s. 
  • Headlong Destruction means that Kravek and any unit that he's attached to gets to improve the armor penetration of their attacks if made against the closest enemy by 1. 
  • Architect of Ruin lets you select a foe to be a hated enemy, and allows Kravek to reroll wound rolls against them, selecting another if he destroys the current one.

Kravek brings along a new unit and an upgraded one to destroy his enemies, the Mutilators, and a new Defiler. Muitiators are demonically warped Chaos Space Marines, close combat version of Obliterators, that can morph their hands into a variety of weapons with which to carry out their sole purpose of engaging the enemy face to face. Their fleshmetal weapons have 3 types of attacks, which lower in number of attacks and increase in strength, from 6x strength 6 attacks, to 2x strength 12 attacks. They can also reroll charge rolls and can deal mortal wounds to nearby enemy units when they charge.

The new Defiler is not only an incredible-looking model, but also a customisable weapons platform. The Defiler comes with 5 ranged weapons and a close combat weapon, and 5 other weapon options that can be swapped with the standard loadout for whatever your destructive needs are. Need a strength 12 -3 AP weapon, you've got the Ectoplamsa Destructor or Hades Lascannon. Need more attacks close up, then the Heavy Baleflamer is for you. Want more attacks in close combat? Then you can change out one of the weapons for an Electroscourge for 5 extra strength 12 attacks.

The Defiler can be built as a generic Chaos/Traitor machine with the Daemonforge ability. This ability lets you reroll a wound roll of 1 when it makes a Dark Pact, which they can do each time it attacks to gain lethal hits or sustained hits. Or you can build your Defiler in service to one of the Chaos gods and their prominent Chaos Space Marine Legions:

  • A Death Guard Defiler can select a unit hit by an attack to not gain the benefit of cover, which means they don't get the bonus to saving throws
  • An Emperor's Children Defiler gains a bonus to the hit roll if the target unit is above half strength
  • A Thousand Sons Defiler can hit targets more easily when firing overwatch, further boosted if the target is near a Thousand Sons Psyker unit
  • A World Eaters Defiler can shoot or charge an enemy that appears within 12 inches of it on the battlefield during your opponent's movement phase

Legions of Steel

Holding the line against the Iron Warriors' attack on their forge world are the forces of the Adeptus Mechanicus. Legions of technologically enhanced warriors in pure service to the Machine God, the polar opposites of the Iron Warriors who've sold their souls to daemons and tainted their technology. On hearing of the plight of Agripnaa, the Archmagos Terminus of Mars, Thulia Ghuld has brought her army in its defence, mainly as her duty to defend the Machine God's assets, but also because she loves to test her creations in the purity of battle, and also because Agripnaa has a wealth of destructive weapons and items that may be of use to her in the future.

Thulia Ghuld can boost a nearby Skitarii unit to allow them to shoot and charge when they advance or fall back, or to activate both Adeptus Mechanicus Doctrina Imperatives, which means that the unit gets a bonus to armor penetration against enemy units in their deployment zone, and the armor penetration of attacks against the Skitarii are reduced if they are within their own deployment zone.

Thulia comes armed with a Jericho-class conversion resonator for ranged combat and the Rod of the Forge for close combat. Both weapons have a high number of attacks, low strength option for cutting through chaff, and a low number of attacks, high strength option for opening up armor. She also has a Secutor of Olympus ability that deals D3+1 mortal wounds to a nearby enemy vehicle on a 2+ at the start of your shooting phase, just to get the party started each turn.

The Archmagos Terminus doesn't come alone. She brings reinforcements in the form of heavily armored Skitarii, the Hastarii. These units come in 2 types, which you have a choice of building out of the Hastarii box. Hastarii Exterminators focus on removing infantry targets. Their Erdication Casters can fire with a 30-inch range with -1 armor piercing, or 15-inch range with -2 armor piercing. They also have an 18-inch ranged Hastarii Arc Blaster with anti-vehicle 4+ and devastating wounds to give them some agency when vehicles get too close. If you want to target vehicles properly, the Hastarii Fusiliers are your go-to unit. Their Neutron Fusils have strength 12, -3 armor piercing, and D6+1 to open up armored foes, and their Monocular Targeting Helms ignore cover when they target a vehicle or monster unit.

Troops aren't all Ghuld brings to the battlefield, a new titan class also walks the galaxy with the new Knight Destrier. These knights bridge the gap between the fast Armiger Knights and the weapons fortresses of the Questoris Knights. The Destrier moves 12 inches, with a bonus of D3 inches to normal or advance moves thanks to their Ram Jets ability. 

You can choose between 4 weapons for your Knight, 2 ranged or 2 melee, or a combination, on top of the fixed Questoris Heavy Stubber. If the Knight takes both melee weapons, you can add 2 to the attacks value, so choosing between 7 attacks at strength 12, with D3+3 damage, or 12 attacks, strength 8 with 2 damage if using the Bellatus Reaper Chainsword. The Thundershock spear has the same or worse stats across the 2 profiles, so you would only use it to gain the Lance ability to make it easier to wound on the charge.

Warhammer 40K Eye of Terror Reign of Iron Final Thoughts?

The Eye of Terror Reign of Iron brings some incredible new forces to accompany the campaign books. The 2 new leader miniatures for Ghuld and Morne both look fantastic, and the difference between them, despite their similarities, is well handled. While the Defiler is also awesome, the Knight Destrier steals the show, especially for the speed at which they can engage and the damage they can do dual-wielding close combat weapons. The lore for the new campaign book is fantastic, and it's good to see Perturabo take the stage for the next big campaign against the Imperium. It's a shame that there isn't a campaign book as we've had with the other 2 previously, as beyond the lore, if you don't want the Apocalypse rules, which aren't for everyone, there's not much else in the book set, and it would have been awesome to have some thematic narrative battles for the Iron Warriors and Adeptus Mechanics to play out to track the war.

The copies of Warhammer 40K Eye of Terror Reign of Iron, the Iron Warriors, and Adeptus Mechanicus used to produce this review were provided by Games Workshop.



https://techraptor.net/tabletop/reviews/warhammer-40k-eye-of-terror-reign-of-iron-review-iron-vs-steel
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
